creator: _jkrow
caption:
When I packed for Antarctica, boots were my biggest question mark 🤷🏼‍♀️ 

Everyone assumes you need thick, stiff bricks on your feet to stay warm, but I’ve learned that the right gear is not always the biggest gear.

I put @vivobarefoot’s most extreme boots, the Tundra ESC, through the wringer when we arrived to the White Continent - wet landings, hours in the snow and ice 

AND I could still feel the ground beneath me. Which I really liked because don’t we all come to Antarctica for a real connection to one of the wildest places on Earth? 

What’s the most extreme environment you’ve tested your gear in? Let me know below! 👇 

🥾 And if you need advice on picking your next every day Vivobarefoot hiking boot, say ‘BOOT’ and I’ll send you the link to my recent Vivo boot comparison video + a lil discount code

transcription.text: out here you layer up like your life depends on it because well it kind of does but the right gear doesn't just keep you alive it changes your entire experience and your boots are one of the most important things you'll put on your body these are the tundra eses from vivo barefoot and i brought them on an antarctic expedition to see if a barefoot boot could actually handle the coldest place that i personally have ever been i wanted to actually feel the ground i was exploring instead of just stomping around in big old bricks that are most regular snow boots and these tundras are designed for deep dry cold so they have a wool felt insole flexible upper and a michelin outsole for traction on ice and snow i wore them every day in antarctica for zodiac rides hikes and freezing temps and hours in the wind and snow i kept my feet warm and dry even during brief wet landings and what surprised me the most is that they still felt like a barefoot like i could move my toes and i didn't lose that connection to the ground beneath me which was really important to me the tundras were built for this type of extreme environment and on this trip they definitely proved it
